title: Fake parcel fee or customs release notice
summary: Victims receive SMS or WhatsApp messages claiming a parcel is held by customs or courier partners and must pay a small fee immediately through a suspicious link.
redFlags: Small fee used to create trust
malaysiaContext: Parcel and customs scams are common in Malaysia, especially when scammers impersonate courier brands or use fake Kastam narratives to push a quick payment.
recommendedActions: Do not pay through the link
Verify the tracking number in the official courier app
Report the sender to your telco or messaging platform

title: Macau scam safe-account transfer
summary: A caller claims to be from police, Bank Negara, or another authority and pressures the victim to transfer funds to a so-called safe account for investigation.
redFlags: Threat of arrest or frozen account
Request to transfer to a safe account
Pressure to keep the call secret
malaysiaContext: The Macau scam pattern remains one of the most damaging fraud narratives in Malaysia because it combines fear, impersonation, and rapid fund transfer instructions.
recommendedActions: Hang up immediately
Call the agency back using its official number
If money moved recently, contact your bank and Malaysia's NSRC 997 quickly

title: Fake KWSP or EPF reactivation link
summary: Scammers claim a KWSP or EPF account is blocked, requires verification, or offers a special payout and direct users to a lookalike login page.
malaysiaContext: Messages tied to KWSP, STR, or government aid work in Malaysia because they exploit urgency around benefits, account status, and trust in public institutions.
recommendedActions: Use only the official KWSP site or app
Never log in through a link from a message
Report the fake domain to your browser safe-browsing channel

title: Bank TAC or OTP phishing message
summary: A message impersonates a bank and asks the victim to confirm an unauthorized transaction by entering a TAC, OTP, PIN, or password on a fake page.
malaysiaContext: Malaysian bank phishing campaigns often imitate Maybank, CIMB, Public Bank, RHB, or BSN and use panic about unauthorized transactions to steal TAC or login credentials.
recommendedActions: Never share OTP or TAC codes
Open the official banking app directly
Call the bank using the number on your card or official website

title: Touch 'n Go eWallet KYC or wallet suspension scam
summary: The victim is told their Touch 'n Go eWallet account will be suspended or limited unless they complete KYC or security verification via a suspicious link.
malaysiaContext: E-wallet scams work well in Malaysia because users are familiar with daily cashless flows and may react quickly if they believe access to wallet balances or QR payments is at risk.
recommendedActions: Open the official wallet app directly
Do not upload your IC or selfie to the suspicious site
Change your password if you already submitted details

title: Telegram or WhatsApp part-time job scam
summary: Users are promised easy commissions for liking content, reviewing products, or completing tasks, then pressured to top up money to unlock larger payouts.
malaysiaContext: Task-based job scams are widely reported in Malaysia because they start with believable side-income offers and condition victims with small early rewards.
recommendedActions: Stop after the first money request
Keep screenshots of the chat and transaction details
Warn others in the community feed without posting personal account numbers

title: Guaranteed-return investment scam
summary: A promoter promises unrealistically consistent daily or weekly returns from forex, crypto, AI trading, or a limited insider opportunity.
malaysiaContext: Investment scams in Malaysia often target retail users with social proof, screenshots of profits, and trust borrowed from financial language or public figures.
recommendedActions: Treat guaranteed returns as a major warning sign
Check BNM and SC investor alert lists before engaging
Do not transfer money to personal accounts

title: Fake utility bill arrears or disconnection notice
summary: Scammers impersonate utilities and threaten service interruption unless a payment is made immediately through a suspicious link.
malaysiaContext: Utility-themed scams exploit routine payment habits and fear of service interruption, especially when the message appears during busy working hours.
recommendedActions: Verify through the official utility app or portal
Do not trust payment links from unsolicited messages
Keep a screenshot for reporting
